research_papers.title: An overview of monitoring methods for assessing the performance of nature-based solutions against natural hazards
research_papers.abstract: showed that salt marshes of Spartina. alterniflora are effective in reducing wave height and energy of 60% to 80%, based on measurements of seawater levels, vegetation height, vegetation density and wave heights. This reduction is non-linear and occurs quickly and the highest at the edge of the marsh and diminishes with distance from the edge. Field measurements and observations of wave energy dissipation effectiveness, compiled by, showed that NBS transect lengths ranging between 10 m and 300 m are capable of reducing the wave height, and thus energy, between 0.3% and 4.0% per metre of vegetated NBS. Similarly, an experimental study by showed that salt marshes can attenuate the water level within the salt marsh at a rate of approximately 600 mm per km of marsh, which falls within the values measured in seven other studies carried out in Europe and the USA. Their study was based on inspections and surveys, as well as continuous in situ measurements and monitoring using pressure transducers, differential GPS and ADCP to capture the storm, sea, vegetation and seabed characteristics. In situ measurements of sea/wave levels and current velocities adequately quantify the depletion rate of wave height inside a mangrove forest used as an NBS against storm surges in various parts of Vietnam. Similarly, measured the depletion rates of peak water level along mangroves, and through coral reefs, during an extreme storm surge event. The disadvantage of in situ measurement and monitoring of the storm surges attenuation is the cost of construction, maintenance, and instrumentation of NBS and adjacent coastal areas as well as the costs of potential damage in an extreme event.

research_papers.title: From marshes to coastlines: A metric for local and national scale identification of high-value habitat for coastal protection
research_papers.abstract: Field data on wave attenuation over saltmarsh in northwestern Europe were extracted from and. The data used from each study were restricted to average wave-height attenuation values, given by the percent reduction in wave height over a marsh transect of some given length, and ignoring spatial and temporal variability in vegetation structure (see Discussion). To incorporate the long-term temporal scale of the studies, which all spanned months, only temporally-averaged values were used. These average values also include a variety of water depths and relative wave heights, factors which are known to influence wave attenuation by vegetation. Some studies also included information on wave energy reduction, which was not included for consistency in data type. Data were extracted both directly from the text and manually from figures. After a literature search and comparison with the literature reviews of and, the data used here includes, to the best of the authors’ knowledge, all field data on wave-height reduction by coastal saltmarshes in northwestern Europe.

Underlying this method is the assumption that wave refraction causes the waves to cross the marsh parallel to the shoreline.

research_papers.title: A trade-off approach to optimize nature-based flood defense designs: riparian willow forests as case study
research_papers.abstract: Here wave height reduction (hereafter also denoted as wave attenuation) was used as a quantitative proxy for flood safety. Estimates were based on the approach of, which calculates wave reduction through vegetated fields considering wave energy losses via two mechanisms: wave breaking (as waves become unstable when approaching shallow coastal areas) and wave energy dissipation by vegetation (due to the physical obstruction) as established by. The wave dissipation term associated to vegetation resistance is integrated over the water column in 1-cm intervals, enabling detailed modelling of vertical variations in vegetation frontal surface area, similar to but with higher vertical resolution. This approach has been validated for wave attenuation by willows and salt marshes, as detailed in.

research_papers.title: Marshes with and without sills protect estuarine shorelines from erosion better than bulkheads during a Category 1 hurricane
research_papers.abstract: Marshes with and without sills presumably provided erosion protection via wave attenuation and stabilization of sediments. Shepard and colleagues conducted a meta-analysis on the protective role of coastal marshes and evaluated the ability of marshes to perform the following functions: wave attenuation, sediment stabilization, and floodwater attenuation. Positive correlations between marsh width and wave attenuation and marsh width and sediment stabilization were found. Additionally, the meta-analysis revealed that marshes less than 10 m in width (which is the width of many fringing marshes found along the NC shorelines surveyed in our study), can reduce wave heights by 80% for waves <0.5 m in height and can reduce wave heights by 50% for waves >0.5 m in height. In terms of sediment stabilization, marshes promoted vertical sediment accretion, reduced sediment loss, and maintained or increased the surface elevation of the shoreline. We acknowledge that wave attenuation abilities of marshes decreases with increasing wave height and because water levels exceeded 0.5 m at our study regions (see ), wave attenuation was likely less than 50% for marsh shorelines in this study. However, given the lack of visible damage and change in surface elevation or vegetation density in comparison to the damage observed to bulkheads within our study regions, we conclude that sills and marsh vegetation stabilized the shoreline despite reduced wave attenuation capabilities of marshes during the storm.

research_papers.title: Developing indicators to identify coastal green infrastructure potential: The case of the Salish Sea region
research_papers.abstract: studied seagrasses and their role in reducing wave energy over a one-meter test section. They concluded that the percentage of average wave reduction by four species of seagrasses was approximately 40% and wave energy reduction was significantly cut when water depths become higher or equal to twice the mean leaf length. found that through a 4 m wide profile of wave tank, wave heights over areas without salt marshes ranged from 71% to 129% more of the height of waves for areas with salt marshes. found that salt marshes reduced wave heights in all observations at rates 27%–98%. They also found that the total energy dissipation rates were on average 82% over salt marshes compared to on average 29% on sand flats. In another study suggested that on average 92% wave height attenuation was obtained over a 310 m coastal profile of salt marshes, and the first 10 m of the canopy provided the most rapid reduction in wave height. Later, suggested that salt marshes could effectively reduce up to 33% of wave height over a 10 m coastal profile, depending on the salt marsh canopy height and density, and water depth conditions.

research_papers.title: Field assessment of wave attenuation by BESE-elements in a mangrove environment
research_papers.abstract: In Experiment 1 (A ), a statistically significant relationship between relative wave height and wave attenuation existed for all configurations; existing vegetation (r = 0.63, p < 0.01), single BESE-Element (r = 0.71, p < 0.01), and bare sediment (r = 0.70, p < 0.01) (A). In addition, existing vegetation and single BESE-Element demonstrated higher wave height reduction percentages compared to the bare sediment configuration for a given relative wave height. The existing vegetation reduced the wave height on average by 15.2 % compared to bare sediment, while the single BESE-Element achieved an average reduction of 2.8 % relative to bare sediment.

research_papers.title: Living Shorelines for Coastal Resilience
research_papers.abstract: The response of salt marsh vegetation to storm conditions is of great interest to property owners considering a Living Shoreline. A recent study utilized an outdoor flume, planted with marsh species native to the North Sea (Puccinella and Elymus) , to measure the wave attenuation and erosion protection of a 40 m marsh over a variety of water levels and wave heights. The combination of marsh vegetation and soil surface was capable of reducing wave energy by 20% during tests run to simulate storm conditions, with wave heights up to 0.9 m. Overall, however, a wetland's ability to attenuate wave energy decreases as water levels exceed canopy height, and storm surge reduction by a kilometer of marsh is on the order of tens of centimeters. During these events, however, the marsh surface is resistant to erosion, and numerous observations have been made of hurricanes delivering sediment to the marsh surface, rather than eroding it (see). These observations are consistent with research concluding that it is frequent lower-energy waves that drive marsh shoreline erosion, rather than isolated large storm events.

research_papers.title: Wave attenuation over a  Scirpus mariqueter  salt marsh during typhoon Muifa
research_papers.abstract: Considering the wave attenuation capabilities of various vegetation types, it is evident that Bulrush salt marshes exhibited the highest attenuation rates, achieving a 60% reduction in wave height reduction at 50 m from SC and a remarkable 98% reduction at a distance of 200 m from the edge. In comparison, Spartina alterniflora and mangrove salt marshes achieved similar attenuation rates, with 98% wave height reduction at a distance of 400 m from SC. These findings suggest that a Bulrush salt marsh covering only one-third the width of a Scirpus mariqueter salt marsh would achieve similar wave attenuation ratios. Furthermore, planting a 200 m-wide Bulrush salt marsh or a 300 m-wide Spartina alterniflora salt marsh along the shore would result in substantial wave attenuation.

research_papers.title: Modeling wave attenuation in salt marshes under variable vegetation and wave conditions using XBeach model with a new parameterization scheme
research_papers.abstract: Herein, the numerical model is validated against experimental data from to assess its ability to predict wave height attenuation by salt marshes under extreme wave conditions. Experiments were conducted in a 300 m long, 5 m wide, and 7 m deep wave flume, with a 40 m long section of marsh blocks spanning the full width. Based on the wave height measurements reported by across the marsh-covered section of the flume, we applied the corresponding wave transmission coefficient (Kt = H i /H 0) in our analysis to assess the model's ability to reproduce wave attenuation by salt marsh vegetation. In the numerical model, the total computational domain was set to 110 m to capture wave attenuation. A one-dimensional uniform grid with a spatial resolution of Δx = 0.025 m was applied, and the total simulation duration was 600 s. The specific test conditions, including wave characteristics and vegetation configurations, are summarized in.

research_papers.abstract: The salt marsh vegetation, on the other hand, is more dependable and has stable wave dissipation effect. Although the wave dissipation capacity of the vegetation decreased as the water level rose, it was still significantly greater than that of the submerged dikes. The wave height reductions generated by the vegetation was 50–80%, 30–60%, and 20–50% in S1, S2, and S3 wave conditions, respectively. Under excessive water levels, salt marsh vegetation can still provide steady wave reduction when compared to engineering measures such as submerged dikes, as demonstrated by the flume experiment.

research_papers.title: Estuarine and Coastal Ecosystems and Their Services
research_papers.abstract: Nevertheless, the coastal protection benefits of salt marsh are significant. Hydrological simulation for eight representative global estuary types indicates that saltmarshes reduced flooding across all studied estuaries and particularly for the largest (100-year) storms, for which they mitigated average flood extents by 35% and damages by 37%. Case study evidence supports the high protection value afforded by salt marshes. During Hurricane Sandy in the US, they reduced property damages along the Atlantic Coast by as much as $625 million. In the Gulf of Mexico, wetland restoration is estimated to yield more than $7 in direct flood damage reduction benefits for every $1 spent on restoration. In Southeast Louisiana, which includes New Orleans, a 0.1 increase in salt marsh continuity per meter of marsh reduces property damages from hurricane storm surge by $99-$133 and a 0.001 increase in vegetation roughness per meter decreases damages by $24-$43. These reduced damages are equivalent to saving 3–5 and 1–2 properties per storm, respectively, in a coastal area containing 1780 households on average. In Wales in the United Kingdom, survey respondents expressed an overwhelming preference for expanding salt marsh with high quality vegetation as a “nature-based solution” defense against coastal flooding and other hazards, with a strong and significant willingness to pay for such schemes.

research_papers.title: Impact-based risk assessment of coastal infrastructure adaptation to climate change: a case study of hurricane risk in Nova Scotia, Canada
research_papers.abstract: The comparative performance of adaptation measures reveals important technical insights into the mechanics of risk reduction. The higher benefit–cost ratios observed for salt marshes indicate that nature-based solutions can deliver effective surge attenuation with favorable economic efficiency. This finding is consistent with growing empirical evidence that ecosystem-based adaptation can outperform or complement hard-engineered defences in terms of cost-effectiveness (e.g., Refs. ). Conversely, the stronger protective performance of sea dikes under high return-period events mirrors results observed in structural flood defence assessments under extreme loading conditions (e.g., Ref. ).

research_papers.title: Experiment-supported modelling of salt marsh establishment
research_papers.abstract: In the face of global warming, the use of ecosystem-based flood defences like salt marshes has become a hot topic. Salt marshes attenuate waves and stabilize the bed, thereby improving coastal safety. Furthermore, they provide a habitat and nursery ground for both rare and commercially important species, filter the water and function as a carbon sink. Because salt marshes trap sediment, they can – within limits – keep up with sea level rise (e.g. ). This is in direct contrast with conventional measures like dikes, which are built at a fixed elevation or even decrease in elevation due to processes like soil subsidence and compaction. Due to the above-mentioned ecosystem services that salt marshes provide and their ability to grow with sea level rise, engineering solutions that incorporate the use of salt marshes may be more effective and cost-efficient than traditional engineering solutions that do not so. This potential has resulted in several (partly overlapping) rationales aiming to actively use the afore-mentioned ecosystem services, such as ecological engineering, building with nature or nature-based flood defence.

research_papers.title: Ecological Approaches to Coastal Risk Mitigation
research_papers.abstract: Historically, hard coastal engineering has been regarded as the solution to protect coastal areas from flooding and erosion. Under increasing threats of sea-level rise, policymakers have to assess physical and economic limits on the height and size of hard defenses. In response to rising sea levels and economic costs associated with building hard defense structures, existing defenses may in some cases be economically less favorable than soft defense. As a result, the use and creation of saltmarshes have recently come to the forefront of coastal defence and policy as a “soft,” sustainable, and therefore cheaper alternative to defending coast lines with hard structures. Therefore, saltmarshes are increasingly managed or even created to protect the foreshore, with the marsh serving as a natural buffer to reduce the forces imposed on and erosion of the seawall or other defense structure, thereby decreasing maintenance and construction costs. Thus, from a coastal defense perspective, the accepted benefits of created or restored saltmarshes are increased wave attenuation and a localized reduction in sea-level rise from an increase in tidal storage. Creating saltmarshes can be cheaper than upgrading or repairing existing defenses; in addition to the economic benefits provided by coastal wetlands, their biodiversity and functional value is now recognized in law under the European Union Habitats Directive. The directive seeks to maintain no-net-loss in total habitat area.

research_papers.title: Top ten priorities for global saltmarsh restoration, conservation and ecosystem service research
research_papers.abstract: To deliver on global habitat restoration goals, areas suitable for restoration must be selected based on cost-benefit analysis of the restoration methods required, whether the areas earmarked for restoration have the potential for long-term success and do not interfere with natural marsh expansion-erosion dynamics, and the value of ecosystem service and biodiversity benefits likely to emerge from the restored habitat, especially for coastal flood protection and carbon sequestration. Tidally restricted coastal areas may need improved hydrological connectivity (e.g., tidal gates dismantled, or upstream dams/levees removed), while bare, degraded, or eroding marshes may need to be rehabilitated through active transplantation of vegetation, invasive species removal, or construction of wave breaks to stabilise eroding shorelines and create windows of opportunity to facilitate pioneer establishment. In all cases, systematic inclusion of positive inter- and intra-species interactions is key to increasing restoration success. Furthermore, the use and values attributed to the hinterland selected for managed realignment must be considered – low-value biodiversity-poor agricultural land, where removal of dykes and levees would lead to natural recolonisation of saltmarsh, may represent an example of high restoration potential.

research_papers.title: Tidal-flat reclamation aggravates potential risk from storm impacts
research_papers.abstract: Most developing countries with a high population density in Asia, including China, Vietnam, Bangladesh, and the Philippines, primarily rely on seawalls for coastal storm protection. Unfortunately, the natural storm flood mitigation functions of tidal flats (i.e., here refer to both unvegetated mudflat and saltmarshes) have been underappreciated. Since the middle of the last century, many of these countries have reclaimed large littoral areas for land demands. The newly reclaimed polders that have been moved further seaward are generally low-lying, and thus highly sensitive to storm impacts. In order to address this issue, China, for instance, had constructed long, hard-engineered defenses of approximately 14,000 km length in total along the 34,000 km-long coastlines to protect its coastal population of roughly 600 million. The benefit of hard-engineered structures in mitigating economic loss and casualty is universally recognized. However, environmental changes of sea-level rise, land subsidence, and record-breaking extreme storm events are eroding the seawall's protective ability. Maintenance costs are hence expected to rise with time. Tidal flats are increasingly recognized as “recumbent seawalls”, providing long-term protection to the conventional hard-engineered defenses. These natural interfaces with the sea have an inherent resilience against sea-level rise and hence, in contrast to human-constructed defenses, do not need structural maintenance, and further provide valuable ecosystem services that vertical seawalls do not offer.

research_papers.title: The use of bioshields for coastal protection in Vietnam: Current status and potential
research_papers.abstract: There are a number of advantages, including cost-effectiveness and long-term service, when using bioshield as a strategy for coastal protection. One of the most important advantages of coastal protection using bioshield is the maintenance of a natural shoreline habitat with its original biodiversity and ecosystem, in addition to ecosystem services as well as natural transport of sediments across coastal areas. On the contrary hard structures (such as seawalls and dykes) obstruct the natural cycling and movement of nutrients which are not effective in nutrient cycling, whereas a bioshield is a good absorber of water during coastal floods and are effective for water nutrient cycle and pollutant filtering. Bioshields do not cause a serious reduction in the aesthetic appearance of shorelines and, hence, the recreational value is not reduced considerably. Vegetated coastal environments are good at stabilizing sediments and coastal forests are effective at reducing wind speed during (low/medium) cyclones. Hard structures have no role in carbon sequestration whereas bioshield ecosystems, particularly mangroves, seagrasses and saltmarshes, are great carbon sinks and hence provide a key role in climate change mitigation. Furthermore, bioshield ecosystems, produce very little energy reflection and wave attenuation by friction, the opposite of hard structures (i.e. hard structures causes wave attenuation by breaking and friction, which is responsible for substrate loss). In addition, a more resilient and climate change resistant foreshore defence can be provided by using a group of complimentary ecosystems (mangroves, salt marshes, seagrasses, coral reefs or oyster reefs) rather than using a single ecosystem.

research_papers.title: Review on processes and management of saltmarshes across Great Britain
research_papers.abstract: Saltmarsh management in Great Britain has changed fundamentally over the last 70 years. Saltmarshes are no longer being exploited for land claim, and are instead being protected and restored at a national level. Reasons for the shift in management thinking are linked to societal concerns about environmental degradation after WWII (especially for bird habitat in the context of saltmarshes), and the need for preserving the numerous ecosystem services that saltmarshes sustain. Marshes now form part of the national coastal flood risk management strategy, and ambitious targets have been set to reverse the national declines in marsh extent by the end of the decade. Scientific understanding of the patterns and processes of marsh change have underpinned the transformation in coastal management policy. Effective monitoring of both saltmarsh extent change and the key processes responsible for marsh loss have enabled a strategic approach for deciding where to focus conservation efforts. Characterisation of coastal units into sediment cells (under the Shoreline Management Planning framework) is enabling managers to predict how coastlines will likely evolve over decadal and centennial timescales, presenting an opportunity for the large-scale roll-out of saltmarsh habitat creation schemes. Important gaps need to be addressed if salt marshes are to be preserved in the long-term: (i) tidal flat extent change and sediment supply monitoring is needed in order to anticipate where marshes are vulnerable to net losses under sea level rise; (ii) forecasting of the long-term ecosystem service value of saltmarshes should incorporate cyclical marsh dynamics which operate over varying spatio-temporal scales, and; (iii) greater effort needs to be made to integrate stakeholders in management decisions, so that differences in the processes and perceptions of marsh change are recognised, allowing the rapid roll-out of effective protection and restoration schemes.

research_papers.title: Salt marshes for flood risk reduction: Quantifying long-term effectiveness and life-cycle costs
research_papers.abstract: This paper aims to quantify the long-term cost-effectiveness of salt marshes in reducing flood risk, in comparison to conventional dike heightening. Several possible human interventions on the foreshore are introduced, aiming to influence its biogeomorphological development and thereby the flood protection level and/or the expected lifetime of the hybrid flood defense. We express the performance of hybrid flood defenses in terms of reduction in probability of dike failure. Flood risk, which is the expected annual damage due to flooding, is generally defined as the product of probability and consequences of dike failure. Although foreshores affect multiple failure mechanisms, only dike failure due to wave overtopping is considered here, since (1) this is often one of the dominant mechanisms for coastal dikes, (2) wave overtopping directly depends on dike crest level and SLR, and (3) it is affected by vegetated foreshores via wave height reduction. The failure probability is calculated in a probabilistic assessment, in which the variabilities and uncertainties in storm, salt marsh, and dike characteristics are taken into account. Cost-effectiveness of different measures is assessed in a life-cycle cost analysis by comparing initial and future investment costs with averted damage.

inner_hits.chunk: This paper aims to quantify the long-term cost-effectiveness of salt marshes in reducing flood risk, in comparison to conventional dike heightening. Several possible human interventions on the foreshore are introduced, aiming to influence its biogeomorphological development and thereby the flood protection level and/or the expected lifetime of the hybrid flood defense. We express the performance of hybrid flood defenses in terms of reduction in probability of dike failure. Flood risk, which is the expected annual damage due to flooding, is generally defined as the product of probability and consequences of dike failure. Although foreshores affect multiple failure mechanisms, only dike failure due to wave overtopping is considered here, since (1) this is often one of the dominant mechanisms for coastal dikes, (2) wave overtopping directly depends on dike crest level and SLR, and (3) it is affected by vegetated foreshores via wave height reduction. The failure probability is calculated in a probabilistic assessment, in which the variabilities and uncertainties in storm, salt marsh, and dike characteristics are taken into account. Cost-effectiveness of different measures is assessed in a life-cycle cost analysis by comparing initial and future investment costs with averted damage.

research_papers.title: Coastal habitat squeeze: A review of adaptation solutions for saltmarsh, mangrove and beach habitats
research_papers.abstract: For instance, saltmarshes and mangroves act as effective flood control measures, potentially reducing annual flooding by > 15 and >20%, respectively. The use of this ‘natural infrastructure’ enables both future coastal habitat protection and coastal risk reduction, and has been shown to be a cost-effective solution compared to more frequently used grey infrastructure solutions such as dikes and levees. The removal of barriers such as weirs, levees and seawalls (through managed retreat) and construction of living shorelines or hybrid green-grey solutions should therefore be given consideration by coastal communities as they develop their responses to the threats posed by sea level rise.

research_papers.title: Interactive effects of vegetation and sediment properties on erosion of salt marshes in the Northern Adriatic Sea
research_papers.abstract: Vegetated coastal habitats such as salt marshes provide multiple ecosystem services, including habitat provisioning, nutrient cycling, climate regulation, and cultural services. The use of salt marshes in ecosystem-based approaches to flood defence is highly promising, as they have been demonstrated to attenuate waves even under storm-surge conditions. There has thus been a focus in recent decades on the conservation and restoration of salt marshes to protect coasts from erosion, storm surges and sea level rise, and to prolong the life of traditional engineered structures such as sea walls and embankments. The perennial saltmarsh grasses of the Spartina genus are of particular interest in coastal protection as they are pioneer species with a global distribution. Their ecosystem engineering properties include trapping sediments from the water column, which enables accretion and establishment at the low marsh.

research_papers.title: Advances in understanding the challenges and opportunities of hybrid sea defence approaches for coastal resilience
research_papers.abstract: Previous studies have focused on examining the performance of vegetated foreshores, seagrass, coastal marshes, coconut trees, mangroves, and oyster reefs, Shepard et al. and van Hespen et al. concluded that salt marsh vegetation and mangroves substantially positively affect stabilising shoreline and wave attenuation. These natural ecosystems not only stabilise shorelines and attenuate waves but also reduce coastal flooding by shortening flood peaks and storing floodwaters associated with coastal storm events. It is worth noting that the wave dissipation of mangroves is mainly attributed to their complex root structure and trunks, which can enhance friction and turbulence dissipation locally when interacting with incident waves. As for the seagrass and salt marsh, the relative swaying motion between stems/blades and water drives the drag and wave kinetic energy dissipation, which is highly influenced by the characteristics of vegetation including blade flexibility and blade height to water depth ratio. Even though the wave attenuation mechanism of oyster reefs which is a complex process of wave breaking, reflection, overtopping, and frictional dissipation is yet to be established, a number of studies have demonstrated the effectiveness of oyster reefs in wave dissipation. Their potential in coastal engineering and climate change adaptation is substantial and should be further explored. However, it is worth noting that the limited hydrodynamic performance under extreme storm events, long-term growth/restoration period, significantly excessive covering area, and unstable performance due to temporal and spatial heterogeneity make it challenging to fully replace the role of hard sea defences in mitigating wave hazards. But these natural elements could work as supplementary measures to the hard sea defences to provide the function of climate adaptability, erosion control, and a higher cost-benefit ratio. Based on the field measurements and numerical simulation, reported that the summer saltmarsh area of 54.2km 2 contributes to the equivalent flood mitigation with the seawall heightening of nearly 0.42 m and provides a higher cost-benefit ratio (by 1.5 times) than the seawall construction. Therefore, combining hard sea defences with vegetated foreshore has become one of the most focal topics in the hybrid sea defences analysis.

These natural ecosystems not only stabilise shorelines and attenuate waves but also reduce coastal flooding by shortening flood peaks and storing floodwaters associated with coastal storm events. As for the seagrass and salt marsh, the relative swaying motion between stems/blades and water drives the drag and wave kinetic energy dissipation, which is highly influenced by the characteristics of vegetation including blade flexibility and blade height to water depth ratio. Even though the wave attenuation mechanism of oyster reefs which is a complex process of wave breaking, reflection, overtopping, and frictional dissipation is yet to be established, a number of studies have demonstrated the effectiveness of oyster reefs in wave dissipation. Their potential in coastal engineering and climate change adaptation is substantial and should be further explored. However, it is worth noting that the limited hydrodynamic performance under extreme storm events, long-term growth/restoration period, significantly excessive covering area, and unstable performance due to temporal and spatial heterogeneity make it challenging to fully replace the role of hard sea defences in mitigating wave hazards. But these natural elements could work as supplementary measures to the hard sea defences to provide the function of climate adaptability, erosion control, and a higher cost-benefit ratio. Based on the field measurements and numerical simulation, reported that the summer saltmarsh area of 54.2km 2 contributes to the equivalent flood mitigation with the seawall heightening of nearly 0.42 m and provides a higher cost-benefit ratio (by 1.5 times) than the seawall construction. Therefore, combining hard sea defences with vegetated foreshore has become one of the most focal topics in the hybrid sea defences analysis.

The integration of ecosystem engineering species (e.g., mussel beds and oyster beds), which can provide benefits of ecological restoration, sand sediment stabilisation and wave attenuation, into coastal protection schemes facilitates dynamic interactions between organisms and the coast. Besides, such integration schemes result in several nonnegligible socio-economic benefits, e.g., additional tourism revenue. Applications of nature-based solutions (NbS) (e.g. manmade dunes and beaches, restoration of coastal wetlands, maritime forests, and oyster reefs) can effectively reduce climate-induced risks such as flooding and erosion while providing many environmental and social benefits such as increased tourism and enhanced fishery resources. In 2013, defined ecological engineering as an integrative approach that leverages interactions among society, ecosystems, and engineering to address coastal hazards caused by global climate change. Such innovative methods have been tested worldwide and gained increasing scientific attention. For example, studied the ecological benefits of ‘living seawall’ in Australia; explored the potential of adopting saltmarshes as complementary structures to traditional seawalls for flood protection in China; proposed a long-term study evaluating the ecosystem restoration function of vertipools in southern England.

The design of hybrid sea defences must balance the internal influences of built structures and natural or artificial habitat elements. reported that the over-engineering design may damage the ecosystem and lead to short-term, unsustainable construction. The coastal squeeze is recognised as a typical negative result of an unideal design; some studies reported a possible method to add some channels to convey sediments and nutrients from inland to the coastal areas to reduce the influences of ‘coastal squeeze’ and satisfy the requirements of long-term stability of habitats. However, the effectiveness of this approach is reported limitedly. Besides, excessively emphasising ecological design may lead to poor wave dissipation performance, especially in storm conditions; for instance, the saltmarsh with seawall has a higher wave energy reduction than the single saltmarsh design. Although a specific area of vegetation could be equally considered as a particular height of the built structure in defencing wave hazards, the spatial and temporal variableness leads to an uncertain coastal protection performance. Thus, cooperation between coastal engineers and biologists is highly suggested to establish suitable hybrid sea defences worldwide.

research_papers.title: Heterogeneity of values for coastal flood risk management with nature-based solutions
research_papers.abstract: There have been some non-market valuation studies on coastal flood risk management that compare man-made defence structures and NbS (e.g., ). There have also been valuation studies focused on increasing the protection or size (e.g, managed realignment) of natural habitat (e.g. ; ). However, these studies often focus on ecosystems as a whole (i.e., on the coast or estuary instead of individual habitats) and have rarely compared values for the different ways in which one habitat can increase flood protection depending on its management. In the case of saltmarshes, recent findings have confirmed that they can reduce flood risk by increasing their area but also by changes in the state of the vegetation. Thus, this study aims to not only assess willingness-to-pay for man-made defence structures vs saltmarsh, but also between two different saltmarsh management practices.

research_papers.title: Safeguarding human health in the Anthropocene epoch: report of The Rockefeller Foundation– Lancet  Commission on planetary health
research_papers.abstract: Intact and restored ecosystems can contribute to resilience, such as the improvement of coastal protection through wave attenuation or the ability of floodplains and greening of river catchments to protect from river flooding events by diverting and holding excess water. Although the effectiveness of ecosystem-based adaptation measures is deemed to be lower than comparable engineered solutions at present, ecosystem-based adaptation measures tend to be implemented at much lower cost and are also often so-called low-regret options because they provide other benefits beyond resilience. For example, in Vietnam, planting mangroves for storm surge protection incurs one-seventh the cost of the creation and maintenance of seawalls or dykes for this purpose and the coastal ecosystem also preserves wetlands and marine food chains that support local fisheries. Care has to be taken with ecosystem approaches, however, to consider and address potential adverse health effects, such as the proliferation of mosquitoes (a potential carrier of vector borne diseases) in restored wetlands.

research_papers.title: Global conservation priorities of coastal habitats towards extreme sea level rise risks
research_papers.abstract: Given the increasing likelihood of escalating flood hazards due to rising sea levels and expanding populations at risk, there is an urgent need to adopt ecosystem-based coastal defenses as a sustainable and cost-effective alternative to traditional artificial levees. Coastal ecosystems, such as tidal marshes and mangroves, can substantially reduce incoming wave heights and reduce both loss and risk. For example, mangroves provide flood protection benefits exceeding US$65 billion per year the worldwide. In the United States, the hazard risk reduction benefits of coral reefs are estimated to exceed US$1.8 billion annually. However, previous global studies that concentrated on specific types of coastal ecosystems often overlook an integrated assessment of the cumulative flood protection benefits provided by these ecosystems. Moreover, although prior studies have demonstrated the effectiveness of coastal vegetation in mitigating flooding risks, the integration of habitat functions with risk analysis on a global scale to inform future conservation priorities has been infrequently pursued.

research_papers.title: Salt marshes for nature-based flood defense: Sediment type, drainage, and vegetation drive the development of strong sediment beds
research_papers.abstract: As a result of climate change, sea levels are rising and storm intensity is increasing in certain regions. This will lead to significant increases in flood risks along coastal and estuarine shorelines. To protect shorelines from sea-level rise and increased storminess, current hard engineering structures for flood defense need to be heightened and strengthened. However, this maintenance is required regularly, hence is expensive and may become unsustainable. Therefore, there is currently a growing interest in complementary measures for flood protection, such as the integration of nature-based solutions with engineered coastal defenses. In particular, salt marshes are considered highly valuable for nature-based flood risk mitigation (e.g. ; ). The active large-scale implementation is however awaiting resolving several knowledge questions.

query: I want you to add multiple sections, being introduction, background, methods and results. The sections should be clearly separated, having one or two paragraphs each one after another. Use info already present in the text, but clearly add more info related. This should have multiple revisions leading to a huge text with multiple paragraphs separated in sections
input_preview: Antibiotic resistance gene (ARG) abundances were quantified in influent and effluent samples collect
input: Antibiotic resistance gene (ARG) abundances were quantified in influent and effluent samples collected monthly over a 14-month period from a municipal wastewater treatment plant serving approximately 250,000 population equivalents. The plant operates a conventional activated sludge process with secondary clarification followed by UV disinfection. Composite 24-hour samples were collected using refrigerated autosamplers, and total genomic DNA was extracted from membrane-filtered concentrates using the DNeasy PowerWater Kit. Quantitative PCR targeting the 16S rRNA gene and six clinically relevant ARG targets—blaTEM, blaCTX-M, mecA, vanA, sul1, and qnrS—was performed in triplicate on a QuantStudio 6 Flex system using published primer sets and thermal cycling conditions. Gene abundances were normalized to 16S rRNA gene copies to obtain relative ARG concentrations. Treatment significantly reduced absolute ARG copy numbers across all targets. Effluent relative ARG concentrations were generally lower than influent values but the magnitude of reduction varied across gene targets.

Adding these sections strengthens coherence because each part serves a distinct rhetorical function, allowing background, rationale, methods, and results to be understood in sequence. The expanded content also increases credibility by anchoring each section in relevant detail rather than leaving gaps the reader must infer. Before proceeding, it may be useful to confirm the intended discipline or topic so the added material aligns accurately with the research area.", "suggestions": [{"original_text": "", "transformed_text": "

Introduction

Antibiotic resistance has emerged as a critical global health concern, with wastewater treatment plants (WWTPs) recognized as important reservoirs and conduits for antibiotic resistance genes (ARGs). Effective introductions should contextualize the research problem and identify the knowledge gap motivating the study, as emphasized in guidance on scientific writing [C12-1-1-1, C12-1-1-3]. Although WWTPs are designed to reduce microbial loads, their role in shaping ARG dynamics remains insufficiently characterized, particularly regarding the variability of removal efficiencies across ARG classes. Establishing this context enables a clear rationale for the present investigation, aligning with recommendations to frame the topic’s relevance and significance early in the introduction [C12-1-1-4, C12-1-2-3].

Background

An effective background section situates the study within the relevant body of knowledge, providing readers with the conceptual and methodological foundation necessary to understand the work [C12-1-2-8]. Prior research shows that ARGs can persist through treatment processes and enter receiving waters, raising concerns about environmental dissemination and potential impacts on public health. Conventional activated sludge systems, followed by disinfection, vary in their ability to reduce ARG abundances depending on microbial community composition, hydraulic conditions, and specific gene targets. Following recommended structure, this background narrows toward the study’s focus by identifying the need for longitudinal assessments of ARG trends across influent and effluent streams in full-scale municipal WWTPs [C12-1-2-5, C12-1-2-7].

Methods

Consistent with best practices for transparent reporting of research methodology [C11-1-2-1; C11-1-2-5], this study conducted monthly sampling over 14 months at a municipal WWTP serving approximately 250,000 population equivalents. Composite 24-hour influent and effluent samples were collected using refrigerated autosamplers, processed by membrane filtration, and subjected to genomic DNA extraction using the DNeasy PowerWater Kit. Quantitative PCR assays targeting the 16S rRNA gene and six clinically relevant ARGs (blaTEM, blaCTX-M, mecA, vanA, sul1, qnrS) were run in triplicate following established primer sets and thermocycling protocols. Reporting methodological choices at this level of detail aligns with guidelines emphasizing reproducibility and clarity in scientific manuscripts [C11-1-2-13, C11-1-2-16].

Results

ARG quantification revealed that treatment processes substantially reduced absolute gene copy numbers across all targets, consistent with the function of secondary treatment and UV disinfection. While relative ARG abundances normalized to 16S rRNA gene copies also decreased from influent to effluent, the magnitude of reduction differed between gene families, suggesting gene-specific persistence mechanisms. As recommended for results reporting, only key findings are highlighted without interpretation, reserving explanatory discussion for later sections [C11-1-2-3; C11-1-2-18].

This chapter explores the essential components of a research report, highlighting the significance of logical flow and clear organization. It includes a thorough explanation of important elements such as the introduction, literature review, methods, results, discussion, and conclusion, providing coherent advice on how to present materials. There is helpful guidance on how to write the report, with an emphasis on addressing the intended audience and upholding academic rigor. The chapter also emphasizes the value of editing and proofreading as essential processes for improving the report's precision, coherence, and clarity. To enable researchers to present their work professionally, strategies for content review, ambiguity removal, and readability improvement are described. By adhering to these principles, researchers can produce reports that effectively communicate their contributions to the academic and professional community. A well-structured research report is crucial for effectively communicating research findings and their implications. This chapter outlines the essential components of a research report, offers guidance on drafting the report, and provides strategies for editing and proofreading to ensure quality.", "doi": "", "citation_key": "", "search_tool": "sc_vector", "referenceId": "C11-1-1-2", "type": "sc_vector", "inner_hits": []}, {"title": "Scripting success: a systematic literature review of the process genre approach for writing in Asian education", "authors": [], "year": "2024", "abstract": "The problem and the aim of the study. In the 21st century, the process genre approach has gained prominence among various writing methodologies. Numerous scholars have scrutinized its impact on writing within diverse educational settings. However, the extensive body of research makes it challenging to distil current theories, dependent variables, instruments and teaching methods for writing in Asian education. By doing so, it aims to uncover contemporary theories, effective practices, and relevant pedagogical techniques, contributing valuable insights to enhance writing education in this context. Research methods. The primary methodology employed in this study is the systematic literature review method, renowned for its rigorous approach to gathering, evaluating, and synthesizing existing research. The process initiates with comprehensive data searching across authoritative academic databases renowned for their scholarly rigour and reliability from 2019 to 2023, specifically Scopus, Wiley, Taylor and Francis, Emerald and Sage. A deliberate preference for these databases over more expansive platforms like Google Scholar underscores the commitment to selecting high-quality and peer-reviewed articles. The review process encompasses several stages, commencing with the meticulous screening of titles and abstracts to ascertain alignment with predefined inclusion and exclusion criteria. Subsequently, the selected articles undergo a thorough examination, enabling a nuanced analysis of their content. The culmination of this methodological rigour culminates in a comprehensive report that distils key findings, ultimately contributing substantively to the scholarly discourse in Asian education writing instruction. Results. Within the confines of this systematic literature review, the researchers have successfully identified and meticulously scrutinised a total of nine pertinent theoretical frameworks that are intricately intertwined with the approach to PG writing. Furthermore, it has been ascertained that a total of 19 scholars have employed 12 distinct dependent variables in their respective research endeavours. The analysis conducted in this study has also yielded a comprehensive categorisation of instruments, which encompasses six distinct categories. This categorisation provides valuable insights into the various methodological approaches that have been utilised by scholars in their research on the topic of PG writing. By means of a rigorous examination of pedagogical and compositional methodologies, this synthesis has unveiled overarching foundational principles, encompassing preparation, modelling, planning, joint construction, independent construction, and revision, thereby encapsulating the comprehensive terrain of PG writing research. Conclusion. In summary, this systematic literature review serves as a highly valuable and indispensable tool for forthcoming scholars who are venturing into the realm of research pertaining to PG studies. The comprehensive findings encapsulate a wide range of interconnected theories and frameworks, a multitude of dependent variables examined, a variety of instruments employed, and intricate observations regarding teaching methodologies employed in the domain of PG writing research. The compilation of essential elements presented herein serves as a valuable resource for scholars seeking to engage in PG writing research. Translation Studies highlight the specificity of legal translation by defining this practice as a particular case of specialized translation which varies according to its operational context. Multilingualism actually implies a new definition of translation as multilingual co-writing. International cooperation studies how clear legal writing can be ensured with respect to the normative and standard-settings activity and the associated diplomatic and political issues belonging to international organisations. Several writing models have been developed since the 1970s, with each new model building on previous ones by addressing points of criticism or expanding components based on new findings. Today’s models encompass numerous factors and provide a theoretical framework for understanding writing; however, their validity has not been sufficiently tested and validated, especially outside the languages and orthographic systems in which they were developed, which is particularly English with its opaque orthography. For writing models to universally explain writing, they need to be applicable in different contexts. One of the biggest challenges in validating writing models is the role of orthographic transparency, on which there is little research. The aim of this paper is to present important milestones and significant changes in the development of writing models that have resulted from criticisms of previous models and efforts to overcome them, as well as to highlight the remaining uncertainties regarding the components and relationships within writing models, especially in the context of their generalization to different languages and scripts (orthographies). The literature review reveals a lack of studies testing the validity of writing models in languages with transparent orthographies compared to English and other opaque languages. Existing research findings for transparent languages such as Croatian, whose features facilitate writing acquisition and automatization of writing skills compared to opaque languages such as English, show a lesser role of transcription in the quality and fluency of written discourse. It is crucial to reflect an organized flow of ideas throughout the different sections of the paper to convey a clear message in a storytelling manner. Simplicity and clarity are essentials. Having a structure for presenting the data makes it easy to deliver the manuscript effectively. The IMRAD style is one of the most used by different publishers: Introduction (Question asked?), Methods (How was it studied?), Results (What was found?), Discussion (What do the findings mean?). The introduction section should summarize the knowledge gap that led to the research question. The method section should state the patient population, methods selection, and statistical analysis used in the study. The result section is the “number section,” where all relevant results should be reported objectively. A suggested flow of the discussion section is to summarize the major findings at the beginning, which will address the questions posed in the introduction. Then, comparing the results with the most recent trials should follow, whether the results supported or declined the current thinking. Grounded in the New London Group’s multiliteracies, Sociocultural/Social Constructivist perspectives, and the Process Writing model, the study follows a Design–Development–Research (DDR Type 2) pathway across three phases: framework design, expert validation, and empirical verification. Using the Fuzzy Delphi Method with domain experts, the Integrating Artificial Intelligence (IAI) construct was endorsed (nine refined items retained), highlighting roles for adaptive guidance, analytics, virtual assistants, content recommendation, academic integrity checks, educator professional development, and ethical safeguards. A pilot with ESL foundation students and a subsequent large-scale Rasch analysis established reliability, item fit, and construct coherence, confirming the framework’s practicality for real classrooms. Focus-group data triangulated quantitative results, showing perceived gains in clarity, coherence, and self-regulated revision via timely AI feedback. The chapter offers implementable guidance for teachers, curriculum designers, and policymakers seeking sustainable, ethical AI adoption in writing instruction and sets a research agenda for adaptive pathways and capacity building.", "doi": "10.4324/9781003665472-7", "citation_key": "", "search_tool": "sc_vector", "referenceId": "C11-1-1-10", "type": "sc_vector", "inner_hits": []}, {"title": "Addressing High-Level Discourse Units beyond the Paragraph: Challenges, Approaches and Materials", "authors": [], "year": "2024", "abstract": "Interest in the segmentation of written discourse has significantly increased in recent years. This phenomenon can be attributed to different factors, such as theorization (e.g., clear categorizations and delineation of units employed in written texts segmentation without relying on traditional syntax, the lack of consensus on the properties behind basic units like paragraph, etc.), didactics (e.g., the growing difficulty in writing formal texts, which has been observed in inadequate textual organization and information distribution, improper use of punctuation and discourse markers, etc.) and the emergence of new hybrid genres where units such as sentences or paragraphs may not completely align with the analyzed linguistic production (e.g., written texts on social media where there is no correspondence between a paragraph and a unit of meaning, or where even classical positions such as initial, medial or final could be altered). Compared to segmentation models of oral discourse, widely developed (Pons, 2014), written discourse models seem to remain in the background despite their theoretical strength. This seems to be the result of an overshadowing derived from the interest in analyzing genres like colloquial conversation or interview. This special issue, composed of five papers based on different theoretical frameworks, aims to shed light on a key issue: organizing, characterizing, and delimiting units above paragraph and below the text, a challenging space for researchers.", "doi": "10.5209/clac.96956", "citation_key": "", "search_tool": "sc_vector", "referenceId": "C11-1-1-11", "type": "sc_vector", "inner_hits": []}, {"title": "Potential Factors Influencing the Rhetorical Patterns of Research Article Discussion Sections", "authors": [], "year": "2022", "abstract": "The present study reports potential factors influencing the rhetorical patterns of research articles (RA) discussion sections. The selected writers were those who had an educational background in language and language teaching. The interviews covered the interviewees’ background information, current activities, writing activities, and their rhetorical patterns of discussion sections. The interviews were conducted by utilizing the snowball technique to search for more information. All data transcription was then categorized and coded. Research findings revealed that the writers’ choice of move structure could be as a result of learning from other people’s rhetorical patterns, believing themselves, having high self-confidence, having high writing frequency, and having high awareness in the micro and macrostructure of writing discussion sections. Here we provide detailed guidance on writing for research, especially for master’s theses and PhD dissertations and primarily for physical geography and other earth science students. The main sections of a research paper (Introduction, Literature Review, Methods, Results, Discussion, Conclusions) each have specific requirements of what to include and exclude. While there are many aspects to research writing, they should be thought of as a process leading to a final product. The proposal becomes a well-developed first draft of the Introduction, Literature Review, and Methods sections, with Results, Discussion, and Conclusions added after the data are collected and analyzed. New information and improvement of the writing lead to the frequent updating of all sections. In addition, Research Notes are fundamentally important to the process of research and students should develop the habit of entering and maintaining high-quality notes. The Supplement is where details are presented that are important but not included in the main text. References need to be taken seriously from the beginning of the project and the Abstract is the last major part to be written but must be carefully crafted.", "doi": "10.1080/02723646.2025.2455754", "citation_key": "", "search_tool": "sc_vector", "referenceId": "C11-1-1-13", "type": "sc_vector", "inner_hits": []}, {"title": "DECIPHERING FRAMEWORKS FOR WORK BREAKDOWN STRUCTURE DEVELOPMENT IN COMPLEX CONSTRUCTION PROJECTS: A SYSTEMATIC LITERATURE REVIEW", "authors": [], "year": "2025", "abstract": "The increasing scale and multidisciplinary nature of complex projects such as data centres or hospitals have amplified the need for structured, encompassing, and effective Work Breakdown Structures (WBS) to ensure successful project delivery. The aim of this research is to conduct a systematic literature review to identify existing WBS development frameworks. Specifically, the study seeks to 1) categorise available guidance, and 2) explore the frameworks for cohesion and applicability. The research method investigates findings from initially fifty-three publications and narrows to thirty-three using online databases, that focus on WBS development and implementation on construction projects. The investigation concludes the available literature falls into three main categories: international standards, project specific, and project management guidance. The findings reveal that standards lack depth for complex projects, while tools like artificial neural networks are narrow in scope and complex to implement. Project management guidance emphasizes scope clarity, however, fail to outline methods in which to develop a WBS for construction and iterative tasks, highlighting fragmented literature and gaps in WBS frameworks, emphasising the need for robust methodologies adaptable to complex projects.", "doi": "", "citation_key": "", "search_tool": "sc_vector", "referenceId": "C11-1-1-14", "type": "sc_vector", "inner_hits": []}, {"title": "Metaknowledge extraction based on multi-modal documents", "authors": [], "year": "2021", "abstract": "The triplet-based knowledge in large-scale knowledge bases is most likely lacking in structural logic and problematic of conducting knowledge hierarchy. Experiment results have proved the effectiveness of metaknowledge elements extraction by our framework. At the end of this paper, we propose and analyze the task flow of metaknowledge applications and the associations between knowledge and metaknowledge.", "doi": "10.1109/ACCESS.2021.3068728", "citation_key": "", "search_tool": "sc_lexical", "referenceId": "C11-1-1-15", "type": "sc_lexical", "inner_hits": []}, {"title": "Undergraduate and Graduate Students’ Challenges: A Qualitative Study with ONDAS Framework Across Multiple Disciplines and Innovative Research Methodologies", "authors": [], "year": "2023", "abstract": "The integration levels among research methods, management, research tools, and psychology could guide the development of the ONDAS framework toward the secrets of knowledge. Some master’s students write doctoral theses, and vice versa, confusing their identified research approach. The study utilizes desk documents to identify the main problems that most students experienced during their research, to determine the knowledge structure through the analysis of benchmark studies, and to achieve the framework’s development foundation. As a result, it proposes ONDAS framework characteristics, procedures, and rules to be applied in different fields through data triangulation. Aiming to help the research process, ONDAS focused the study from its very beginning until viva voice discussion; this framework decreases the time of writing and preparation, guiding the research and strategy processes, and promoting tools to help with the analysis and elaboration of final theses. Results were achieved by a qualitative approach using the case study method that includes 40 participants in a semi-structured questionnaire on their experiences with ONDAS framework. This study is seen as the first of its kind to assess participants' attitudes, especially their research journey that serves as the primary way of adoption.", "doi": "10.46743/2160-3715/2023.6679", "citation_key": "", "search_tool": "sc_vector", "referenceId": "C11-1-1-16", "type": "sc_vector", "inner_hits": []}, {"title": "Metadiscourse in academic writing: A systematic review", "authors": [], "year": "2023", "abstract": "A means to control how writers mark their presence, negotiate knowledge claims, and engage with their audience, metadiscourse is one of the most prominent approaches to analysing academic writing. The present systematic review attempts to take stock of the existing literature by investigating how metadiscourse has been researched in academic writing by analysing a sample of 370 high-quality empirical studies published between 1990 and 2021. Studies were coded for their conceptual frameworks, research designs, data sources, study contexts, writers, texts, corpora, and reporting practices. It was found that over 80% of research involved cross-sectional descriptive corpus-based analysis, drawing on intercultural rhetoric. Owing to its impact, ease of application, and study comparability, most research adhered to the ‘broad’ tradition in metadiscourse. Representative of this approach, Hyland's interpersonal framework and models of stance and engagement were prevalent, although difficulties in undertaking a ‘thick’ analysis of such a wide variety of features coupled with publishing constraints meant that many authors narrowed their focus to a few select features (especially hedges, boosters, and self-mentions). Approximately 37% of corpus-based research followed the ‘thin’ tradition, with an emphasis on marker frequency counts over contextually-bound interpretations. Corpora of English texts, notably, research articles, were prominently studied, with little research taking place outside of university contexts or recruiting human participants as informants. We discuss avenues to advance research in metadiscourse, through identifying possible future inquiries and improving study quality.", "doi": "10.1016/j.lingua.2023.103561", "citation_key": "", "search_tool": "sc_vector", "referenceId": "C11-1-1-17", "type": "sc_vector", "inner_hits": []}, {"title": "INSTRUCTION IN CRITICAL WRITING ELEMENTS", "authors": [], "year": "2026", "abstract": "How can teachers help their students to achieve clarity and impact in a piece of writing? This chapter introduces the section Designing Writing Lessons 3: Building the Structure and Content of Writing. The structural elements it explores include crafting a thesis (statement) and developing effective paragraphs that clearly communicate the writer's ideas. Teachers are pointed towards helping their students to understand the importance of the function that each structural element of a piece of writing is intended to fulfil in order to achieve the desired effect on the readers. Included subtopics are: Introductions Thesis Statements Paragraphs Conclusions", "doi": "10.4324/9781003619154-13", "citation_key": "", "search_tool": "sc_vector", "referenceId": "C11-1-1-18", "type": "sc_vector", "inner_hits": []}, {"title": "Multilevel term analysis for adaptive document filtering", "authors": [], "year": "2020", "abstract": "Humans tend to organize information in documents in a logical and intentional way. This organization, which we call textual structure, is commonly in terms of sections, chapters, paragraphs, or sentences. This structure facilitates the understanding of the content that we want to transmit to the readers. However, such structure, in which we usually encode the semantic content of information, is not usually exploited by the filtering methods for the construction of a user profile. In this work, we propose the use of term relations considering different context levels for enhancing document filtering. We propose methods for obtaining the representation, considering the existence of imbalance between the documents that satisfy the information needs of users, as well as the Cold Start problem (having scarce information) during the initial construction of the user profile. The experiments carried out allowed to assess the impact, in terms of T11SU measure, on the filtering task of the proposed representation.", "doi": "10.3233/IDA-200006", "citation_key": "", "search_tool": "sc_lexical", "referenceId": "C11-1-1-19", "type": "sc_lexical", "inner_hits": []}, {"title": "PaperBridge: Crafting Research Narratives through Human-AI Co-Exploration", "authors": [], "year": "2025", "abstract": "Researchers frequently need to synthesize their own publications into coherent narratives that demonstrate their scholarly contributions. To suit diverse communication contexts, exploring alternative ways to organize one's work while maintaining coherence is particularly challenging, especially in interdisciplinary fields like HCI where individual researchers' publications may span diverse domains and methodologies. In this paper, we present PaperBridge, a human-AI co-exploration system informed by a formative study and content analysis. PaperBridge assists researchers in exploring diverse perspectives for organizing their publications into coherent narratives. At its core is a bi-directional analysis engine powered by large language models, supporting iterative exploration through both top-down user intent (e.g., determining organization structure) and bottom-up refinement on narrative components (e.g., thematic paper groupings). Our user study (N=12) demonstrated PaperBridge's usability and effectiveness in facilitating the exploration of alternative research narratives. Share your writing with others to determine if you are as clear and consistent as possible in your description of the methods.", "chunksource": "body", "paragraphid": "p0050", "sectionid": "s0010", "section": "Writing About the Methods to be Included in a Research Proposal", "normalised_section": "Methods", "chunkid": "B9780128156803000189-chunk-9"}]}, {"title": "Symposium: Essentials of scientific writing for publications", "authors": [], "year": "2025", "abstract": "The following paragraphs highlight some thoughts about each section in a manuscript, presented in the recommended order of writing. These suggestions should be tailored to the individual writer's preferences and specific requirements of the manuscript. 1. Tables and figures. It is advisable to spend an adequate amount of time on creation of tables and figures, adhering closely to the author guidelines specified by the journal for table and figure formats. Failure to follow guideline details may result in reviewer dissatisfaction. Tables and figures should be logically aligned with the description of the experiment and treatments as written in the materials and methods section and fit the desired approach in the results and discussion sections. In this manner, the paper will assume a clear and logical flow which increases overall readability. 2. Results. The results section can be likened to a movie where the reader is presented with the full scope of findings. However, much like in film editing, not all results may need to be included in the final manuscript. Unnecessary data from assays with no clear relevance may be omitted after deliberation with a supervisor. Similarly, consider whether negative results should be omitted from the manuscript. Certain negative results may not always contribute to the narrative; however, some negative results may hold valuable insight. A logical order should be maintained when referring to tables and graphs to ensure smooth transitions between sections. 3. Materials and methods. The materials and methods section can be compared to a detailed baking recipe. Just as anyone with no baking experience should be able to replicate a recipe by following clear instructions with specific measurements, so should readers be able to replicate a research based on the materials and methods section. Clarity is key in this section, including good writing mechanics, using proper grammar, structure, and flow. Seek to describe the research work as clearly as possible, and in a way that aligns with how the data are presented and described in the tables and graphs and results sections. A helpful self-assessment is to consider whether someone could accurately diagram the experimental design based on the materials and methods section. 4. Discussion. The discussion section can be among the most difficult sections to write, depending on the writer's level of comfort. It can be likened to a movie critique, whereby after reviewing a movie (results section), an analysis, interpretation, and comparison of other works in the field is discussed and implications and next steps are presented. When writing the discussion section, consider the readers and the level of detail and expertise that matches the journal's target audience.", "doi": "10.1016/j.psj.2025.104989", "citation_key": "", "search_tool": "sd_fulltext_vector", "referenceId": "C11-1-2-3", "type": "sd_fulltext_vector", "inner_hits": [{"chunk": "The following paragraphs highlight some thoughts about each section in a manuscript, presented in the recommended order of writing. Understanding the context of the results, for example, depends on a clear and robust methods section. A strategy to achieve a cohesive manuscript is beginning the writing process by generating a Need-Answer-Discovery (NAD) statement, inspired by principles of biodesign. First, write one sentence articulating the medical need. Guiding questions are: what specific patient outcome needs improvement? What intervention needs testing on its efficacy? Next, write one sentence capturing the significance of the conducted study in addressing this need. Finally, summarize the findings in a single sentence: what was discovered? illustrates a suggested flow for NAD statement creation and generation of the primary manuscript elements from abstract through conclusion.", "doi": "10.1016/B978-0-323-90391-2.00098-1", "citation_key": "", "search_tool": "sd_fulltext_vector", "referenceId": "C11-1-2-4", "type": "sd_fulltext_vector", "inner_hits": [{"chunk": "Before writing, it is important to consider how the sections connect on a macroscopic level. Similar to a blueprint, the research method section provides a guide for how a study was performed. describes the research methodology as the most important aspect of a study because its contents provide information used to assess the study's validity and credibility of its results. In other words, a clear methodology assists the reader in understanding how the results were obtained, how to interpret study results, and how studies can be replicated and translated into practice.", "doi": "10.1016/j.pedhc.2024.05.001", "citation_key": "", "search_tool": "sd_fulltext_vector", "referenceId": "C11-1-2-5", "type": "sd_fulltext_vector", "inner_hits": [{"chunk": "The traditional “methods and materials” section of a research paper describes the research protocol used to conduct a research study. Similar to a blueprint, the research method section provides a guide for how a study was performed. describes the research methodology as the most important aspect of a study because its contents provide information used to assess the study's validity and credibility of its results. In other words, a clear methodology assists the reader in understanding how the results were obtained, how to interpret study results, and how studies can be replicated and translated into practice.", "chunksource": "body", "paragraphid": "para0005", "sectionid": "sec0002", "section": "RESEARCH METHODS PAPER: DEFINITION", "normalised_section": "Methods", "chunkid": "S0891524524001007-chunk-2"}]}, {"title": "Writing the research paper—The methods section", "authors": [], "year": "2025", "abstract": "This chapter presents information detailing the composition of the Methods section. This section is frequently not given the attention it deserves, but provides the technical foundation and support for the Results and Discussion. The merits of a particular study and its ultimate candidacy for publication will often be heavily influenced by impressions with respect to the Methods section. A five-paragraph format for the Methods is suggested, with sections dedicated to description of the study substrate, study specific definitions and protocols, technical considerations, experimental details, and statistical analysis. The study substrate paragraph should provide support as to what makes your dataset or study source appropriate to answer your study question, or why it should otherwise be viewed as novel. For any studies with an intervention, the technical section is where the nature of the procedure is described. This is also the paragraph where any novel surgical approaches should be discussed in the setting of a clinical case series. The experimental details section provides information on the means through which any experiments were conducted, or how specific tests were administered. For research involving radiographic measurements, the modalities used in these assessments and the exact ways in which the relative figures were developed will be disclosed here. The statistical analysis section may represent the largest proportion of the Methods in retrospective studies and research involving clinical registries, healthcare claims, or large databases of similar nature. In randomized trials, basic science investigations, and biomechanical studies, it may be shorter and contain more limited details. While ample use of technical appendices to supplement the Methods section is encouraged, I caution against trying to save space by referring to previously published protocols or other research, as a shortcut to providing adequate detail in the Methods. Following these recommendations resound to your benefit, as a Methods section that is easy to work through and understand will result in favorable ratings on the part of reviewers and also increase the likelihood that readers recommend your paper to their colleagues once it appears in print. A Concise Guide for Novice Researchers and Reviewers", "authors": [], "year": "2024", "abstract": "Transparency in research involves being candid and open about the research process. It is a practice in which researchers provide honest details about how the research was done, including our analytical and interpretative choices visible in a way that allows the reader to evaluate them. These choices need to be aligned with the approach and analytic position employed. Researchers should ensure procedural rigor by being explicit in describing the way research was conducted through a detailing of how participants were recruited; how rapport and trust were developed; describing how data were collected, recorded, coded, and analysed; notes on interactions with collaborators and participants; and sharing accounts of how errors or participant refusals were dealt with. The researcher should therefore disclose every aspect of the data collection process, and the guidelines used to analyse data, by presenting excerpts of the textual data which affords the reader to discern their analysis and/or make available a record of the data to other analysts or the level of detail in transcription. Some authors refer to this as an audit trail of notes and research content (e.g., interview guide, transcripts, audiotapes) that documents how the research was done. These can also be recorded in a diary, reflexive journal or memos and codebooks (when codebook methods are used). This information should be included throughout the account of the research, (e.g., described in the Method section but most evidence is included in the Appendices), or in content presented in case additional information about the research is required. This process also obliges the researcher to disclose the challenges and unexpected developments in the study, as well as revealing how the research focus transformed over time.", "doi": "10.1177/16094069241282843", "citation_key": "", "search_tool": "sd_fulltext_vector", "referenceId": "C11-1-2-7", "type": "sd_fulltext_vector", "inner_hits": [{"chunk": "Transparency in research involves being candid and open about the research process.
