The citations and reference list in were created from a .tex file in an Overleaf project using the template for ACM SIGCHI conference papers. The papers were included in a separate .bib file in BibTeX format. As an example, shows the BibTeX entry for the conference paper in. See the Wikipedia entry for “bibtex” for further details.

research_papers.title: Manuscript preparation
research_papers.abstract: There are several options for citation managers to facilitate bibliography formatting (and reformatting, in the case of resubmissions), including Zotero, EndNote, Mendeley, and RefWorks. Each program has unique features and has varying costs. What these all have in common is the ability to “cite while you write,” that is, to add citations easily while the manuscript is being written. These programs also offer the ability to reformat references to the standards of most journals, and automatically renumber citations in the bibliography automatically. Most of these programs allow for the creation of shared libraries and can integrate into cloud-based word processing systems, such as Google Docs, which can assist in the process of collaborative writing. An additional feature of citation management software is the ability to store and sort articles. Most citation managers also allow the user to store PDFs of articles, and many allow the user to add notes about each article, allowing for the creation of an annotated bibliography.

research_papers.title: Unveiling the ethical void: Bias in reference citations and its academic ramifications
research_papers.abstract: Addressing citation bias is not straightforward and requires awareness and active measures to ensure that all relevant research is fairly considered and cited. The international scientific community should compile their reference lists with the same seriousness and integrity as they apply to all other aspects of their research. From the perspective of editors and publishers, a possible strategy could be to send manuscripts to reviewers without including the authors' names and affiliations. This practice would help ensure an unbiased review process. In addition, research journals might consider including reference list reviewers, similar to how they employ special statistical and technical reviewers. We feel that this is not an overstatement, given the significant impact citation distortions can have on the progress of knowledge.

research_papers.title: Referencing and Citation Styles
research_papers.abstract: Writers and creators at all levels, in all disciplines, can appropriately incorporate standard referencing formats that add consistency across works of an academic nature and scholarly publications, especially those that are peer reviewed. Thus, it helps them contribute to large conversations among an academic community, making the content easier to understand and digest. Ultimately, referencing ensures credit is given to the appropriate person or entity of an intellectual or creative work, which helps researchers and other contributors build on the research and ideas of those who came before and motivate them to do more and to do so, ethically.

inner_hits.chunk: Writers and creators at all levels, in all disciplines, can appropriately incorporate standard referencing formats that add consistency across works of an academic nature and scholarly publications, especially those that are peer reviewed. Thus, it helps them contribute to large conversations among an academic community, making the content easier to understand and digest. Ultimately, referencing ensures credit is given to the appropriate person or entity of an intellectual or creative work, which helps researchers and other contributors build on the research and ideas of those who came before and motivate them to do more and to do so, ethically.

research_papers.title: How accurate are citations of frequently cited papers in biomedical literature?
research_papers.abstract: Our findings, along with previous studies demonstrating that citation inaccuracies are common, have several important implications for authors. Authors should adopt good citation practices, including those outlined in, when preparing manuscripts. These practices are important for all types of publications, including review articles, which were more likely to contain citation inaccuracies in our study. Inclusion of full texts of all citations in reference manager libraries should become prerequisite. Practices such as sharing libraries and asking multiple authors to check and confirm each citation may help to prevent common inaccuracies, including citations of non-existent findings and inaccurate interpretations of research findings.

research_papers.title: Reference management practices of students, researchers, and academic staff
research_papers.abstract: Taught students (undergraduate and taught postgraduate) tend to manage their references in a purely functional way. This is illustrated by the fact that the most common software mentioned by taught students was Word. Most of the comments indicated a fairly simple process of keeping a list of references to use, or those already used, in a Word document or occasionally in a spreadsheet. Some described simply leaving all electronic sources open in browser tabs until ready to write a reference list. This indicates a purely functional approach to referencing, with several noting they saw this as a “finishing touch” at the end of their work before submitting, rather than something that informed their work on an ongoing basis. This could be a reflection on the context of how and why they are working. Taught students are not building a knowledge base to refer to later, but are primarily using references in a functional way, to support short-term assignments. “I put my references in a separate document in the order they'll appear in my report, then put them in alphabetical order before submitting the report or essay” (42, UG, SCE). For postgraduate researchers (PGRs), comments about their referencing practices were noticeably more detailed and complex than taught students. PGRs are much more likely to use reference management software, but some still keep manual lists. These respondents commonly describe a combination of approaches, and trial and error approach to finding techniques that work.

research_papers.title: Designing a historically grounded bibliographic model to enhance reference reliability in planning research
research_papers.abstract: Constructing a bibliography and a reference list in scholarly journals serves several essential functions in academic writing. They ensure academic integrity, allowing readers to verify sources, trace the evolution of ideas and terms, and assess the validity of the research. References also situate a study within the broader scholarly discourse, demonstrating the authors’ engagement with existing literature and acknowledging prior contributions. Furthermore, well-structured bibliographies enhance the credibility and transparency of research, facilitating reproducibility and intellectual collaboration across disciplines.

They ensure academic integrity, allowing readers to verify sources, trace the evolution of ideas and terms, and assess the validity of the research. Furthermore, well-structured bibliographies enhance the credibility and transparency of research, facilitating reproducibility and intellectual collaboration across disciplines.

research_papers.title: Literary runaway: Increasingly more references cited per academic research article from 1980 to 2019
research_papers.abstract: Many researchers believe that the change in citing behavior, leading to increasingly longer lists of references, is deeply rooted in the perceived benefit of gathering future citations [–,]. As impact factors and performance indicators have long been an intrinsic part of academic publishing, academics have become ever more desirous to publish in prominent journals and to be cited more often. Having more references in their papers becomes a handy style because it not only allows greater visibility in literature networks (i.e., citation consciousness ) but also engenders more academic connections that encourage future mutual citation (i.e., tit-for-tat ). However, naïve writers may feel obligated to review much of the literature and have long reference lists simply because the authors of the papers they read do the same (i.e., herd behavior ). Proactively or grudgingly, authors have made a collective effort to raise the bar for the number of cited references ever higher over time, which has ever-increasing consequences. In our opinion, despite growing numbers of references being fashionable, or even politically advantageous, the fundamental rules of academic paper writing should always be respected and observed: to list only relevant and significant references.

research_papers.title: Literature citation and writer identity construction in linguistic academic discourse
research_papers.abstract: It is acknowledged that obtaining membership of the academic community requires the competence to observe the norms and conventions of the community and communicate within the community. Citation, as one of the academic conventions, enables writers to construct their identity as a member of academic community. Writers in the corpus all use one of the standard referencing systems for in-text citations as well as end-of-text references. This indicates that they possess a proper comprehension of the academic norm of using citations in their work. What's more, more than just acknowledging the owners of the sources, writers strategically use citations for different intentions. As in, the writer employs the cited author's taxonomy to serve his/her study, which reveals the citation type statement of use, and in, the writer applies the adjective “reified” from the source to describe the feature of the register precisely, reflecting the function of application. These two examples show that the writers approximate the expectations of an academic community by adopting other scholars' work or concepts to support their own studies or arguments. Example 1 By employing elements of Barnett and Ceci's (2002) taxonomy … [JA22] Example 2 … a more metaphorical and ‘reified’ (Halliday & Matthiessen, 2014, p. 710) register … [JA15] In addition, it is also common for writers to express praise of specific works or arguments created by well-known authors in the field (see ). Through positive evaluation achieved by the attitudinal markers “ground-breaking” and “rigorous”, it seems to show the writer is “interacting directly with sources” and communicating within the community, which positions himself/herself as an insider to the discipline. And it's worth noting that writers cite their own prior research to support the point they are making, as illustrated in. The self-citation presents the writer as a full member of the academic community as it is an important means of demonstrating one's disciplinary credentials. Example 3 Halliday's ground-breaking theories (Halliday & Matthiessen, 2014) provide a rigorous framework … [JA15] Example 4 … results in the realization of several communication goals (Tankó, 2016) … [JA02] (2) Identity as a contributor to a field of knowledge Not just simply retelling the existing knowledge, writers deploy citations to “transform knowledge”.

research_papers.title: Geochemical databases
research_papers.abstract: Yet even if data sources are referenced and provided with every data download—as is the case with all data systems described in this chapter and indeed is a requirement of the FAIR principles—many journals are still unwilling to include overly long reference lists in the main article, forcing authors instead to include these data citations in a supplementary document that usually is not indexed by Scopus or GoogleScholar and is not automatically linked to the manuscript. In addition, authors are not always aware of these citation requirements, sometimes only opting to name the data system in their manuscript without any further references or indeed any documentation of how they subselected data from a database. This practice means that not only is there no due credit given to the authors who originally generated and published the data, but it is also not possible to reproduce these datasets to verify the scientific outcomes. There are many examples of this behavior in the literature citing the GEOROC and PetDB databases. Another related example are citations to data processing software such as Glitter, which again usually only mention the software name but do not cite the original authors of the reference material values used within the software application.

research_papers.title: The citation catastrophe: Propagation of AI-generated counterfeit citations in scholarship
research_papers.abstract: Automated tools are helpful, but just as they are no substitute for overall human oversight, they cannot assist in the same way for references that do not include a DOI or URL (although they can pull them out of a larger list of references as a secondary list for humans to follow up on). For those there is currently no substitute for persons with editorial responsibilities checking to verify the existence of the citation. The authors recognize the unfortunate additional burden this creates, since many editors of academic journals, like their article authors, are academics not compensated for their work. It is also worth noting that, along with issues at the editorial layer, the promulgation of these counterfeit citations points to serious issues with authors choosing to pad their publications rather than use the literature in a meaningful way in their work.

research_papers.title: Overcompliance and reluctance to make decisions: Exploring warning systems in support of public managers
research_papers.abstract: In order to exapt the practices of managing citations and references from scientific academia to the context of public management, it is important to observe how the information generated by this practice is used to control quality and improve scientific academia in general. The first aspect of using the list of references from articles for decision making occurs when the initial analysis is conducted by the editor, known as a desk review. The list of references in the article can evidence: low levels of relevance to the topics covered by the journal, if it presents a list of journals with little in common and removed from those that explore the same core theme as the journal; and outdated text, identified by analyzing the years of publication in the list of references. In other words, it can be a source of input and support for different analyses. Many repositories of journals, when carrying out the process of uploading a scientific article through the article submission system, even present the author or the person who submits the article with an analytical opinion of the article's reference list. This opinion usually separates the references into two lists: the references identified as indexed in repositories of scientific articles, and those that are not indexed.

research_papers.title: Analyzing referencing patterns in grey literature produced by influential global management consulting firms and international organizations
research_papers.abstract: The simplest aspect of referencing practices that can be examined using citation analysis is the number of sources referred to in a document. The use of references to back up key assertions demonstrates that authors are well-situated in the literature, provides credibility to their viewpoints, and can lend support to their claims and findings. Indeed, as students are taught formal academic writing, they are often provided with guidelines around the minimum number of sources they must rely on to teach them informational literacy, and how to establish credibility.

research_papers.title: Predicting verb forms in reporting and reported clauses: A corpus-based study of academic citations
research_papers.abstract: Citations are central to academic writing and argument, characterized by “the attribution of propositional content to another source” (p. 341) and the representation of the attributed source by the writer. Citations involve a wide range of citational and linguistic aspects, such as the number of sources, content types, citation forms, reporting subjects, reporting verbs, and reporting voice (e.g., ). One indispensable aspect involved in citations is the use of verb forms (i.e., time-reference forms) to express the time reference of attributed propositions. It is, of course, the writer who manages the choice of citational and linguistic features available to them in order to “represent and (thus inevitably interpret) the views of the attributed author” (p. 17). In the case of verb forms, for example, the writer can choose at least three common forms to represent reported sources temporally, namely the simple past (past), present perfect (perfect), and simple present (present) (e.g., ).

research_papers.title: Sources of Information
research_papers.abstract: Referencing means acknowledging where the source of information is taken from, whether it is via traditional or non-traditional sources. References are usually documented in the form of a list. Citation, that is in-text citations, is the way of acknowledging the source of information within the body of the research work. Referencing must be documented by selecting and using an appropriate reference style. The reference style selected will determine the format of the reference and the in-text ciation. Referencing and citing is an essential part of the scholarly process, and there are several reference styles, such as Modern Language Association (MLA), American Psychological Association (APA), Harvard and Chicago Manual of Style.

research_papers.title: Visualization and interaction techniques for single-text digital reading: A survey
research_papers.abstract: To manage citation overload, systems like CiteSee (see (5)) and QuickRef. (see (4)) focus on the sub-category of. CiteSee also employs the technique from to distinguish citation status, while QuickRef uses the technique (part of ) to provide structured abstracts. Beyond managing citations, effectively organizing and synthesizing the paper’s content itself is equally crucial for knowledge construction. To this end, Threddy by Kang et al. is a prime example of the technique within the category. It allows users to highlight text fragments and organize them into hierarchical research threads, thereby facilitating systematic information synthesis. Finally, to foster deeper engagement, the CReBot system by Peng et al. (see (3)) exemplifies the technique, also part of, by generating critical questions to guide readers.

query: Move the final sentence to the end of the Introduction.
input_preview: Introduction: Microplastic contamination of freshwater systems has emerged as a significant environm
input:
Introduction: Microplastic contamination of freshwater systems has emerged as a significant environmental concern, with recent surveys documenting particle concentrations ranging from tens to thousands of particles per liter in rivers, lakes, and drinking water reservoirs across both industrialized and developing regions. The ecological and toxicological implications of chronic microplastic exposure for freshwater organisms remain incompletely characterized, though laboratory studies have reported adverse effects on feeding behavior, reproductive output, and inflammatory biomarker expression in several fish and invertebrate species at environmentally relevant concentrations.

Methods: Water samples were collected at eight stations along a 340 km longitudinal transect of the river during baseflow conditions in June and again during elevated discharge following autumn rainfall in October. At each station, triplicate 50 L surface grab samples were filtered through stacked stainless-steel sieves (500 µm, 100 µm, 20 µm mesh), and retained material was digested in 30% hydrogen peroxide at 60°C for 72 hours to remove organic matter. Suspected microplastic particles were identified and categorized by morphotype (fiber, fragment, film, pellet) under a stereomicroscope, and a random subset of 10% of particles per sample was subjected to polymer identification by micro-FTIR spectroscopy. Blank controls consisting of filtered deionized water processed through identical equipment were run alongside each sampling event to quantify procedural contamination.

Results: Mean microplastic concentrations were highest at the two stations immediately downstream of the major urban wastewater discharge points, with values of 847 ± 213 and 762 ± 188 particles per liter at the 100 µm fraction during baseflow, declining to 234 ± 67 particles per liter at the most downstream rural station. Fibers constituted the dominant morphotype across all stations and both sampling periods, accounting for 58–73% of total particle counts, followed by fragments (18–29%) and films (4–11%). Autumn high-discharge sampling yielded statistically higher total microplastic loads at six of eight stations compared to baseflow conditions, consistent with resuspension of deposited particles from benthic sediments during elevated flow velocities. Polymer composition was dominated by polyester (41%), polyethylene (23%), and polypropylene (17%), with minor contributions from polystyrene, polyamide, and acrylic.

Discussion: The downstream attenuation gradient observed during baseflow is consistent with dilution and settling as dominant removal processes in the absence of additional point source inputs, a pattern reported in several European river systems. The pronounced increase in microplastic loads during high-discharge events suggests that river sediments act as a temporary sink that is periodically remobilized, complicating efforts to use single time-point surveys as representative measures of contamination levels. Seasonal hydrological variability should therefore be considered an important sampling design parameter in future freshwater microplastic monitoring programs.
